sentences.text: Yehudah Bakon.
In Mahrisch-Ostrau, today Ostrava, in Czechoslovakia, Moravia.
Well, it was a town at the border of Silesia, Germany, Poland, and Moravia.
And I was born to a family of Jews who came, immigrated in the First World War from Poland to Czechoslovakia.
And there was a small population of Jews, about, I would say, 7,000 Jews.
I remember when the transport went to Theresienstadt, there were only four transports, which means around 3 and 1/2, 4,000 Jews went from Ostrava to Theresienstadt.
But many, of course, immigrated before.
No, it was an industrial center, coal mines and steel factories.
In 28 of July, '29.
My father was Yisra'el, and my mother was [?
Ethel-- ?] Ester.
Well, they called him, [?
Sroel, ?] Yisra'el.
And my mother was called [?
Ethel. ?]
I had two sisters, Hani and [?
Rela ?], today Rifche.
Rifche was lucky and could immigrate in '39, shortly after the German occupied Czechoslovakia.
And Hani--
To Israel.
And I was left with my sister and parents, and later on came to Theresienstadt and to Auschwitz with them.
The atmosphere was of a small, bourgeois, you would call it today lower-middle class.
We had a tiny little factory with the brother of my father for leather goods.
And we were, you would say today, Masortim.
I went, Saturday, to the synagogue, not all the time, but to all high festivals.
Not always.
But we went to a Jewish school.
It was like a normal school, but mainly the difference was they were for Jewish children.
And we had modern Hebrew.
And the language was Czech and German, depended on who just occupied Mahrisch-Ostrau.
In the time when it belonged to Czechoslovakia, the main language was Czech, and the secondary language was German.
And later on German was the main subject and Czech was the second language.
But of course, we also studied modern Hebrew.
Yeah, yeah, in the beginning--
A Jewish school.
Not a Zionistic school, but there--
Yeah, yeah.
And we--
Well, I thought they knew Hebrew.
But when I came to Israel, I hardly could speak.
I could read.
And I could understand a bit of Hebrew, of course.
My parents were Zionists, because my uncle was even the president of the Mizrachi movement.
And his--
In Ostrava.
And his wife was the president of the women organization, something like [NON-ENGLISH] you would call it today.
And--
My father wasn't an active Zionist, but he was a Zionist, of course.
And we had the blue box a little Keren Kayemeth.
Yeah.
And--
We had knowledge about Palestine, as I remember very well the first Palestine, or film about Palestine in about '35.
And we went even to a kind of Jewish organization, like [NON-ENGLISH].
These were the two organizations.
I was too young to join them, really.
Oh, yes.
They played with this idea.
But of course, I was a small child.
And I only know it-- that it was very urgent when things became not so normal, I mean after '34.
Yes.
But still, your sister Rifche, she had immigrated to Palestine before--
Yes.
No, no, no, under the Germans.
Under the German occupation.
In the beginning it was still possible.
She was in the age of [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH].
And she was about, I think, 10 or 11 years or something like that.
And she came to Israel.
She was-- was she oriented Zionistically?
She was-- Through my parents, yes.
We were oriented in Zionism.
No.
She came just [NON-ENGLISH].
She was in a kind of a moshav or kibbutz.
She was a member of a kibbutz.
She's now of a moshav?
Masu'ot Yitzhak.
Yes.
I remember very well my grandfather and grandmother from the mother's side, because they were living in the same building as we.
And I remember also my grandmother from the mother's side.
My grandfather from the mother's side died before I was born.
And I, of course, care his name, Yehudah.
Uncles?
One uncles was staying in the same-- from the mother's side, in the same time in Mahrisch-Ostrau.
And the brother of my father was also staying in Mahrisch-Ostrau.
And they shared the same fabrik together.
Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] was just around the corner.
Yeah, yeah.
We were in the same school later on.
Yeah.
Only with the children we played on the courtyard of our house.
And these were interrupted, of course, in '39 when the German occupied Mahrisch-Ostrau.
Not before the occupation.
Only the antisemitism started, at least in Mahrisch-Ostrau, around '38, when we heard about what's happening in Austria, the so-called Anschluss.
Then there was a fascistic Czech movement.
And we saw the first kind of posters and signs on the street-- Jews out, or Jews go to Palestine, things like that.
No, it only started in '39.
And somehow, I was not so badly hurt as the other one, because my face, at that time, wasn't so Jewish-looking.
Yes, light eyes.
I remember when there was the thing-- the thing with the Anschluss-- the occupation of Sudeten, that we run away.
But it was only for a couple of days.
And then we came back.
But we only run away to another place to Slovakia.
We went to Slovakia.
I think it was around '38.
Then we came back, because the mobilization, at the time they called it.
There was a mobilization.
And nobody knew what will happen.
But after the German occupied the [NON-ENGLISH], what was called then.
Area of the Sudetenland.
Things calmed down till '39.
And therefore, we ran away.
But we came after--
--a couple, maybe even two or three days, we came back.
I remember we stood with somebody who was taking a kind of job in our factory.
And some officer in-- how you would call it today?
A merchant who worked for us in Slovakia.
And he gave us his flat.
That's what I have in the memory.
Not so much, because we heard all kinds of rumors, mainly from Poland and also from people--
I think the rumors we heard were people who ran away from Austria.
And one of the first memories are that my father came once back, he came usually for lunch.
And he told us what a strange thing happened to me.
Some men came to the factory and asked for help, because Mahrisch-Ostrau was, as I told you, on a border.
And they run away through Mahrisch-Ostrau to Poland, which was, at that time, a way to run away to other countries.
And he asked for some financial help.
Most probably, the Jewish community sent him to me.
And I gave him the money or whatever he wanted.
But how strange.
Why did he have to tell me this story that he was a banker, and now he is penniless?
Anyhow, I gave him the money, which meant that this story that he was a banker, some-- at that time, somebody very rich and so on-- And he had to run away.
When did-- where was this man from?
From Vienna?
From Vienna.
That was, even in his eye, unbelievable.
We couldn't imagine things like that, that this kind of brutality could happen.
It was a Jew, of course.
And my father helped him.
And that was the story which was--
Yeah, around the table at lunch time.
It was shocking.
Yeah, that he couldn't understand it, why he had to say it after he got the money.
He was a refugee from Vienna who came over.
Yes.
Well, did you see any-- andy refugee children in your town?
We saw a few children, which actually came-- I don't know if it was '38, maybe it was even a little later-- to our class.
Because, as I told you, Mahrisch-Ostrau was a place where people passed for one reason or another, to escape.
And the very first time into our class came German children, Austrian children.
Jewish children, because we were a Jewish school.
And it was strange in our eyes.
Suddenly different children with a different kind of mentality, and of course, language.
They didn't know so well Czech.
And that is, well, I remember it very well.
We absorbed them as much as we could.
And I remember even that these children, later on, were helped.
They got free meals.
Every day they went to a different family and got a meal.
Cold lunch.
They were with their parents, of course?
They were usually only with their mothers, for one reason or another.
I can't remember why.
But somehow they didn't have any more of their fathers.
No, I only knew that they had to run away.
And that was the beginning, of course.
Well, this is--
--quite in my memory, because it was the 14, I think, of March, '39.
And suddenly, we saw the first thing was that I saw trains and trains and trains loaded with soldiers.
And they were very huge in my eyes.
Later on, I knew that they sent only the high, I mean, a meter, 80 or so--
--soldiers to make an impression.
And Mahrisch-Ostrau was even one day before Prague occupied.
Prague was a day later occupied.
And I remember there were many refugees from Slovakia and other places.
Even one uncle came and was very nervous, and said the German are occupying Czechoslovakia.
And there was a great tension, and he was running away to another place.
But for us children, it was interesting what's happening We were living could not so far away from the railway station that was looking there.
And then we went out in the street.
And there were some tanks and slogans, Recht gehen links ubertrat, which means, Sonny, go right and cross on the left side.
What did it meant?
In Czechoslovakia, it was different, like today, still in England, everything was on the left.
Oh, so they changed it.
One of the first things was right and left.
The second thing was, as a child, we run to the post office, and we wanted to get the first issue of--
The new stamps.
And the stamp, I got it.
And that was 'die Stadt der schwarzen Diamanten dank den Fuhrer fur ihre befreiung.' Which meant the city, or the town, of the black diamonds, coal, thanks the Fuhrer for their liberation.
And then we went to the streets and saw tanks, and we touched them.
Actually, it was armored cars.
Why?
Because in the rumors, they said, oh, no, they are not really armored.
The German don't have so much armors.
They are from cartoon.
And I wanted to prove it for myself.
Paper, that is it was--
If it's a cartoon or paper or real armor.
It was a real one.
And it was real.
It was real armor.
And that was the first impression.
And slowly, we get to feel the hand of the occupation.
I mean, from day to day, things went from bad to worse, more and more restriction.
The antisemitism was on the high.
There was more and more slogans.
In Ostrava-- In Ostrava there were quite a large population of German, real German.
Well, they were German.
And they went, even before, with the white socks and there was a special movement.
And next to us was the German theater, which I knew before, because my father liked opera, and sometimes I went with him to the next building, which was the German opera and theater house.
And suddenly these German were more and more noisy and started with showing off all kinds of propaganda, windows with the newspaper, NSDAP, or however it was called.
And we saw pictures of Sturmer.
And as a child, I was curious, and I looked on this terribly caricatures against Jews.
Yeah.
On the streets.
Yes, I knew German.
Of course I knew what's happening.
Well, in '39, I was exactly 10 years.
And you knew German?
And I knew German.
I could read it, yeah.
--the caricatures, where they put the Stumer?
Even the German special--
Gothic-- we even wrote in--
Yeah, I could read it very well.
Yeah.
Yeah, we knew.
Well, we were more and more restricted.
And soon came all kinds of rumors about Poland, what's happening there.
Soon we had to give away our-- a room in our flat.
We had to rent it.
And I remember that room we rented was a German moved in.
Yes.
At the beginning--
At the beginning it was-- it wasn't taken.
It was still, in a way, normal.
We rent the room.
One was a German woman, [?
Florian ?] was her name.
A very nice woman.
No, she was, in my eyes, an elderly woman.
Maybe she was 35.
Because later on, her son came to visit us, a soldier.
And I have a story about him.
But in the beginning, everything was more or less normal, but we were forbidden slowly to go to parks-- Jews and dogs.
And--
[INAUDIBLE] are forbidden.
It came later.
First of all, we were forbidden.
The park was written to Jew and dogs, Eintritt verboten.
It's forbidden to go in.
We were not allowed to go to cinemas or to swimming pools and other places.
Later on, there were rationalized the food.
And the Jews could only buy much later and only in certain shops.
And so when we came in the evening, we got only the leftovers.
Still later, we didn't got the same rations like other people.
No milk and so on.
More and more restriction.
Then, there was a law.
We had to give away all our jewelries and pets and bicycles and so on.
Yeah, but at that time, no many people had radios.
And more and more restriction.
Then there was something very strange.
Mahrisch-Ostrau was the very first city or town which evacuated, which there was a forced evacuation of Jews to Nisko.
And this was in '39.
Nisko is in Poland.
And they sent all the Jews, about from 18 to 45 or something like that, had to register and were sent to Nisko.
And that was the beginning of deportation.
It was one of the first--
The-- the beginning of a kind of deportation of Jews.
And Eichmann was the leader of this--
Was in charge of this operation.
Operation, but--
No.
It was built.
There was nothing there.
But the Jews from Mahrisch-Ostrau, they called it Umschulung.
They want reeducate them or re-- give them other profession.
No, it was '39, soon after the occupation.
Well, that was something very curious.
My father said, I don't need another occupation.
I have a good occupation.
I can do things from leather and so on.
And he hit himself.
I mean he didn't went to the call.
And I remember--
No, only men.
And only men who were not, maybe, high officials in the Jewish community, were not teachers.
They were not in this.
But they took all the men.
Yeah, he was in that category, but just he didn't went to it.
And it was, of course, very dangerous.
And he called a Czech doctor just to have a kind of--
Certificate.
That he has a cold or so.
But it was, of course, just a bluff.
And after only a week or so, he slowly went on the street.
Again, he showed himself, and we were very, very afraid, first of all, that somebody could denounce him.
And we knew there are people, even some of the Jews, who were denouncing-- who were working with the Gestapo.
And I remember even the story that somebody met him on the street and said, Mr.
Bakon, you are alive?
I heard that you died in Nisko.
And there were all kinds of rumors, of course.
Did any from-- anyone from your family go to Nisko, your uncles?
No.
From my-- because--
No, no.
Only-- friends, of course, all my--
No.
My friends from the school, their fathers were in Nisko.
And I remember the very first day when we came to school, our teacher gave us a talk and said, now, poor children, you are a half-- you have no father.
And all of us were very sad.
And I had to play that even my father is there.
Because, yeah, I had to pretend.
And at that time, it wasn't so dangerous.
And somehow nothing happened.
And--
A rest, yeah.
